denial_letter_text:
Dear Ms. Member,

Aetna has reviewed the prior authorization request for vaginal hysterectomy with apical suspension for pelvic organ prolapse, submitted on your behalf. After review, the request is denied at this time.

Plan policy requires that a less costly treatment modality be attempted before surgical repair of pelvic organ prolapse. A vaginal pessary is an appropriate and lower-cost modality for the management of prolapse and is required as a first-line approach before surgical correction will be authorized. The requested surgical procedure represents a more costly modality than the plan considers appropriate at this stage.

This determination was made by Dr. R. Patel, a family medicine physician, applying the plan's medical policy. The notice does not provide the reviewer's specialty qualifications for this procedure, a license number, or any record of the time spent on this review.

You may pursue a pessary trial and resubmit the request if it is unsuccessful, or you may appeal this determination.

If you disagree with this determination, you may file an internal appeal by contacting Member Services at the number on your identification card within the timeframe stated in your plan documents. You may submit additional clinical information with your appeal request, and a different reviewer will evaluate the appeal.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Utilization Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 64-year-old woman with symptomatic stage III uterine prolapse, with the cervix protruding several centimeters beyond the hymenal ring, vaginal mucosal erosion, and obstructed voiding that requires manual reduction. Her gynecologist requested vaginal hysterectomy with apical suspension. A vaginal pessary was already fitted and trialed over three months but was repeatedly expelled and caused worsening mucosal erosion, so it is not a viable modality for her. The denial nonetheless requires a pessary trial as a lower-cost modality before surgery, despite this documented pessary failure. The determination was issued by a family medicine physician rather than a gynecologic surgeon, and the notice provides no license number and no record of time spent, so the reviewer's qualifications and identity cannot be verified against the state medical board.
